title: Revolutions of 1848 sweep Europe
summary: In the spring of 1848 a wave of insurrections rolled across the European continent from Sicily in January through Paris, Vienna, Berlin, Milan, Venice and Budapest, toppling the July Monarchy in France, forcing Metternich into exile from Vienna, prising liberal constitutions from the Prussian and Austrian crowns, and briefly proclaiming republics in Rome, Venice and elsewhere. Within eighteen months, the revolutions had been suppressed by armies loyal to the old dynasties. Yet the springtime of the peoples, as contemporaries called it, dismantled serfdom in Central Europe, planted parliamentary institutions from Copenhagen to Turin, and rewrote the terms of nineteenth-century politics.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Vienna settlement of 1815 had reimposed monarchy and aristocratic order on a Europe transformed by twenty-five years of revolution and war. Under it censorship, standing armies, ecclesiastical privilege and dynastic sovereignty were meant to hold back the twin currents of liberal constitutionalism and national self-determination that the French Revolution had loosed. For a generation the system had bent to accommodate crises: the Greek war of independence, the July Revolution in France in 1830, the Belgian secession from the Netherlands the same year, the Polish rising of 1830 to 1831, and a wave of Chartist agitation in Britain in 1839 and 1842. By the mid-1840s the pressure was mounting toward something more general.

Economic conditions were exceptional. The potato blight that struck Ireland in 1845 spread to the continent in 1846 and 1847, wiping out the staple crop of tens of millions of the poor from Belgium to Bohemia. Grain harvests were bad in the same years. Prices doubled and tripled; food riots broke out from Berlin to Palermo. A commercial and financial crisis in 1847 spread from British railway speculation to French banks to German textile mills; unemployment surged in the industrial towns. Peasant grievances against surviving feudal dues and labour services combined with urban artisan complaints about mechanisation to produce a mass base for political change.

On top of the economic distress ran the political demands of a generation that had come of age reading Alexis de Tocqueville, Karl Marx and Giuseppe Mazzini. Middle-class liberals wanted constitutions, parliaments, free press, religious tolerance and civil equality. Radicals and republicans wanted universal male suffrage, popular sovereignty and social reform. Nationalists wanted to unify divided peoples: Italians looked toward a single kingdom or republic, Germans debated a Kleindeutschland or Grossdeutschland solution, Poles dreamed of restoring the partitioned commonwealth, Hungarians pressed for autonomy from Vienna, Czechs, Croats and Romanians for recognition within a reformed empire. The Communist Manifesto of Marx and Engels, published in London in February 1848, opened with the famous sentence about a spectre haunting Europe. In the winter of 1847 to 1848 the political banquets of the French reform movement, held to circumvent a ban on public meetings, converged with all of these currents to make revolution imminent.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The trigger came in Sicily, where an insurrection against the Bourbon king of the Two Sicilies broke out in Palermo on 12 January 1848 and quickly forced the granting of a constitution. In Paris the crisis of the banquet campaign erupted on 22 February when the government banned a scheduled meeting; students and workers threw up barricades in the Latin Quarter, the National Guard refused to fire, and on 24 February the seventy-four-year-old king Louis-Philippe abdicated and fled to England disguised as an English gentleman. A provisional government led by the poet Alphonse de Lamartine proclaimed the Second Republic, abolished slavery in the French colonies, decreed universal male suffrage and set up National Workshops to relieve unemployment.

The news from Paris raced across Europe in days. In Vienna on 13 March students and workers rose in the streets; Prince Metternich, chancellor since 1809 and the architect of the post-1815 order, resigned that evening and slipped out of the capital in a laundry cart, reaching London on 20 April. The Emperor Ferdinand promised a constitution. In Berlin on 18 March barricade fighting killed some three hundred people; King Frederick William IV rode bare-headed through the crowds three days later behind a black-red-gold flag and promised to lead the movement for German unification. In Milan on 18 March the Cinque Giornate drove the Austrian garrison of Field Marshal Radetzky out of the city; Venice proclaimed the Republic of San Marco under the lawyer Daniele Manin on 22 March. King Charles Albert of Piedmont-Sardinia declared war on Austria on 23 March, opening the First Italian War of Independence. In Hungary the Diet at Pressburg under the leadership of the Protestant lawyer Lajos Kossuth voted through on 15 March the April Laws, a package of constitutional reforms that in effect made Hungary an autonomous kingdom sharing only the monarch and foreign policy with Vienna.

A parliament of the German states convened at the Paulskirche in Frankfurt on 18 May 1848 to draft a constitution for a united Germany. Similar national assemblies met in Vienna, Prague, Palermo and Kremsier. The revolutions reached even into the small principalities: Denmark under the new king Frederik VII granted a constitution and became a hereditary constitutional monarchy in June 1849; the Swiss Confederation, which had just fought a brief civil war in 1847, adopted a federal constitution modelled on the American in September 1848; the Netherlands accepted a liberal charter drafted by Johan Rudolf Thorbecke.

The counter-revolution began almost as soon as the revolutionaries had triumphed. In Paris, the closure of the National Workshops on 21 June provoked the June Days uprising of the eastern working-class quarters; General Louis-Eugène Cavaignac crushed the rising in four days of street fighting that left more than four thousand dead. In Prague, General Windischgratz bombarded the city into submission on 17 June. In Milan, Field Marshal Radetzky, having withdrawn to the fortress Quadrilateral, defeated the Piedmontese army at Custoza on 25 July and reoccupied Milan on 6 August. In Vienna, a second rising in October was suppressed by Windischgratz in the last days of the month with two thousand dead; the young emperor Franz Joseph, only eighteen, replaced his uncle Ferdinand on 2 December. In Berlin the king's troops reoccupied the city in November and imposed a conservative constitution in December. The Frankfurt Parliament finally offered the imperial crown of a united Germany to Frederick William of Prussia in April 1849; he refused, calling it a diadem picked up from the gutter. The Hungarian republic declared on 14 April 1849 was crushed by a combined Austrian and Russian invasion; General Arthur Gorgey surrendered his army to the Russians at Vilagos on 13 August. The Roman Republic of Mazzini and Garibaldi fell to a French army sent by Louis-Napoleon on 3 July 1849. The Venetian republic held out under Manin, ravaged by cholera and Austrian bombardment, until 24 August 1849. By the autumn of 1849 the old order was everywhere restored.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Judged by the immediate political outcome, the revolutions failed. The German unification project of the Frankfurt Parliament collapsed; the Italian war ended in Austrian victory; the Hungarian republic was extinguished by Cossack cavalry; Louis-Napoleon Bonaparte, elected president of the French Second Republic in December 1848 with the votes of the peasantry, dissolved the Legislative Assembly by coup on 2 December 1851 and had himself proclaimed Emperor Napoleon III a year later to the day. Constitutional charters granted in the panic of March 1848 were rescinded or emasculated in Prussia, Austria and the smaller German states. Tens of thousands of revolutionaries went into exile, many to Britain, Switzerland and the United States, forming the German Forty-Eighter emigration that would supply so many officers to the Union armies in the American Civil War.

Yet the concrete gains were substantial. Serfdom, the surviving robota and grain dues, was abolished in the Austrian Empire by the imperial patent of 7 September 1848; the emancipation was made permanent by the March Patent of 1849. Serfdom likewise ended in the German states west of the Elbe and in the Danubian Principalities during the revolution and its aftermath. Constitutional government was consolidated in Denmark, the Netherlands, Piedmont and Belgium; the Statuto Albertino granted by Charles Albert of Piedmont in March 1848, alone among the constitutions of the year, survived until 1948 and became the basis of the constitutional monarchy of united Italy. Even in Prussia, the constitution imposed by Frederick William IV in December 1848 and revised in 1850, though heavily conservative, retained an elected Landtag and formed the framework of the North German Confederation and eventually the German Empire.

Most importantly, the revolutions demonstrated that nationalism, once the property of romantic poets and secret societies, was now a mass political force that no European state could ignore. Piedmont, defeated in 1848 and 1849, resumed the leadership of Italian unification under Count Camillo Cavour and completed it in 1861. Prussia, having refused the Frankfurt crown, achieved a similar unification under Bismarck between 1866 and 1871. The Hungarian settlement of 1867, the Ausgleich, transformed the Austrian Empire into the dual Austria-Hungary and gave Budapest most of what Kossuth had demanded in 1848. Karl Marx, watching the collapse of the revolutions from London, concluded that the bourgeoisie had failed and that the working class must lead the next revolution alone.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The revolutions of 1848 have often been called the turning point at which modern Europe failed to turn. The liberal, parliamentary, national and social movements they contained would eventually reshape almost every European state, but they did so under conservative auspices and by conservative means: through the professional armies and the diplomatic manoeuvring of Cavour and Bismarck rather than through the barricades of the springtime of the peoples. The exiled Forty-Eighters, from Carl Schurz in Wisconsin to Alexander Herzen in London to Louis Kossuth in Turin, kept a memory of that spring alive across three continents.

The events of 1848 produced a new political vocabulary. The words left and right, drawn from the seating in the French Chamber, spread through Europe as the essential coordinates of political life. The distinction between liberals and socialists, blurred in the enthusiasm of February, sharpened in the June Days and became foundational to the Marxist tradition. The struggles over the Kremsier constitution in Austria and the Frankfurt Parliament in Germany gave the first sustained European debate on the rights of national minorities within multi-ethnic states.

In France, the memory of the June Days scarred the workers' movement for a generation; the Paris Commune of 1871 would in part be an attempt to redeem it. In Germany, the failure of Frankfurt confirmed the Prussian conservative view that unification, if it were to come, must come by iron and blood rather than by parliamentary resolution. In Italy, the memory of Milan's Cinque Giornate, of Manin's Venice, of Garibaldi and Mazzini in the Roman Republic, became the moral capital of the Risorgimento. In Hungary the honved army, the martyrs of Arad executed on 6 October 1849, and Kossuth in exile were canonised in the national memory.

Historians still argue about whether 1848 was a beginning or an ending, the birth of modern mass politics or the last spasm of romantic revolution. Certainly it marked the point after which no European government could rule for long without at least the fiction of representation, and after which the great empires of the centre, the Habsburgs, Hohenzollerns and Romanovs, lived in awareness that the peoples they ruled could rise. When those empires finally fell in the crisis of 1917 to 1918, the successor states looked back to the constitutions and national programmes drafted seventy years earlier for their founding texts.

worldContext.text: The United States concluded the Mexican-American War with the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo on 2 February 1848, acquiring California, New Mexico, Arizona, Nevada, Utah and portions of four other future states in exchange for fifteen million dollars. Nine days earlier, on 24 January, gold had been discovered at Sutter's Mill on the American River in California; by 1849 the gold rush had drawn some three hundred thousand migrants to the Pacific coast. The Seneca Falls Convention on women's rights, organised by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott, met in upstate New York on 19 and 20 July.

The Qing empire under the Daoguang Emperor was still absorbing the shock of defeat in the First Opium War six years earlier. Silver continued to drain from the country; opium imports through the treaty ports were rising; and secret societies in the southern provinces, including the God-Worshipping Society founded by the failed examinee Hong Xiuquan in Guangxi, were beginning to organise. Within two years Hong would proclaim himself the younger brother of Jesus Christ and launch the Taiping Rebellion, the deadliest civil war of the nineteenth century. In Japan the Tokugawa shogunate under Ieyoshi continued its policy of sakoku, but American whalers and Russian embassies were beginning to appear off the coasts.

British India was completing the annexation of the Punjab. The Second Anglo-Sikh War, opened by the murder of British agents at Multan in April 1848, culminated in the decisive battle of Gujrat on 21 February 1849 and the formal annexation of the Sikh empire on 29 March. The young Maharaja Duleep Singh, ten years old, was exiled to Britain and eventually converted to Christianity. Governor-General Lord Dalhousie, whose Doctrine of Lapse annexed a series of princely states through the following years, laid the groundwork for the Indian Rebellion that would erupt in 1857.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Abdulmecid I was in the middle of the Tanzimat reforms, the great programme of centralised modernisation begun by the Gulhane edict of 1839. In 1848 the empire hosted large numbers of Polish and Hungarian revolutionary refugees, whose extradition Russia and Austria demanded and Britain and France refused to allow, contributing to the tensions that would produce the Crimean War in 1853. Muhammad Ali of Egypt died in 1849; his hereditary dynasty continued to rule Egypt under nominal Ottoman suzerainty.

In West Africa the abolitionist movement bore fruit in the French Second Republic's decree of 27 April 1848, drafted by Victor Schoelcher, which freed some two hundred and fifty thousand enslaved people in Martinique, Guadeloupe, French Guiana and Reunion. In southern Africa the newly proclaimed Orange River Sovereignty extended British authority north of the Orange in February 1848; the Boers under Andries Pretorius rose in resistance and were defeated at Boomplaats on 29 August. Along the Nile the Egyptian modernisation programme continued to draw European engineers and merchants toward Cairo and Alexandria.

In New Zealand the wars between the settler government and Maori communities in the north had been suspended by the peace of 1846, but tensions over land sales were mounting toward the Taranaki and Waikato wars of the 1860s. In Australia the gold rushes that would follow the Californian discoveries were still three years away; the colonies remained dominated by wool exports and pastoral expansion into land taken from Aboriginal peoples. The Rev. Charles Strong's antislavery agitation in Britain paralleled the abolitionist policies emerging simultaneously in metropolitan France.

figures.name: Louis-Philippe I
figures.role: Citizen King of the French, overthrown by the February Revolution in Paris and forced into exile in England

figures.name: Klemens von Metternich
figures.role: Austrian chancellor since 1809, forced to resign and flee Vienna during the March 1848 uprising

figures.name: Lajos Kossuth
figures.role: Hungarian lawyer and orator who led the April Laws, the declaration of Hungarian independence and the failed revolutionary republic

figures.name: Giuseppe Mazzini
figures.role: Italian nationalist theorist and revolutionary; triumvir of the short-lived Roman Republic of 1849

figures.name: Giuseppe Garibaldi
figures.role: Guerrilla commander who defended the Roman Republic against the French army in 1849

figures.name: Louis-Napoleon Bonaparte
figures.role: Nephew of Napoleon I, elected first president of the French Second Republic in December 1848 and later Emperor Napoleon III

figures.name: Frederick William IV
figures.role: King of Prussia, who first paraded under the tricolour and then declined the imperial crown offered by the Frankfurt Parliament

images.caption: Lamartine rejecting the red flag before the Hotel de Ville, Paris, 25 February 1848. Oil on canvas by Henri Felix Emmanuel Philippoteaux. Musee des Beaux-Arts de la Ville de Paris (Petit Palais).
images.credit: Henri Felix Emmanuel Philippoteaux (1815 to 1884), via Wikimedia Commons
